title: The Dungeon Dive's Top 50 Games of All Time* - part 3
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AmYVHRf2Aq8

Numbers 30 through 21 — the upper-mid tier. Daniel (@dungeondive) starts with **Secret Unknown Stuff: Escape from Dulce**, a deceptively simple zero-to-hero crawl whose 3D towers look great but are sometimes more setup than they're worth. **Fortune & Glory** (29) is the pulp-Indiana-Jones globetrotter with so many rules that fan-made flowcharts are essentially required.

**Runebound 2nd Edition** (28) earns its spot over 3rd on the back of its expansions — including fan-made quest decks you can order from Printer Studio. **Dungeon Crusade: Book One** (27) gets the highest praise for tile art on the entire list, though Daniel admits to running a smaller homebrew setup because the published footprint is enormous and sessions can run twelve-plus hours. **Four Against Darkness** (26) appears with the candid admission that the core book alone gets repetitive — the game lives in its forty-odd booklet expansions.

**Hellboy** (25) dropped off the top ten not because it got worse but because Mantic's last Kickstarter left a sour taste. **Darkest Night 2nd Edition** (24) is the classic Pandemic-style threat-manager with twenty-eight characters. **Rogue Dungeon** (23) is the all-card crawl that punches above its small box. **Warhammer Quest: Shadows over Hammerhall** (22) gets called "better HeroQuest" — the game everyone slept on while clamouring for HeroQuest's return. **A Touch of Evil** (21) closes with its absolutely-dripping Hammer-horror cosplay aesthetic.

----

Daniel keeps coming back to length as the recurring negative — even games he loves run too long. Is there a game in this batch you'd actively prefer at half its current runtime?
